items.title: ADP-DiT: Text-Guided Diffusion Transformer for Brain Image Generation in Alzheimer's Disease Progression
items.abstract: ADP-DiT is a novel Diffusion Transformer designed for generating subject-specific, longitudinal Alzheimer's Disease (AD) brain MRI scans. It achieves clinically interpretable control by encoding follow-up time intervals and multi-domain patient metadata (demographics, diagnosis, neuropsychology) into a natural-language prompt. This prompt is processed by dual text encoders (OpenCLIP and T5) to guide the image synthesis, allowing for time-specific progression modeling beyond simple diagnostic stages.
items.figure_caption: The overview of the proposed ADP-DiT framework. The model conditions on multiple text-encoders text embeddings and structured metadata to guide the denoising process in the latent space.

items.title: CausalDisenSeg: A Causality-Guided Disentanglement Framework with Counterfactual Reasoning for Robust Brain Tumor Segmentation Under Missing Modalities
items.abstract: CausalDisenSeg addresses robust brain tumor segmentation under missing MRI modalities by employing a Structural Causal Model (SCM) framework. The core method uses causality-guided disentanglement to explicitly separate the anatomical Causal Factor from the modality-dependent Bias Factor using a CVAE with an HSIC constraint. This allows the model to learn robust segmentations by relying only on the causally relevant features, even when some modalities are absent.

items.title: MedRCube: A Multidimensional Framework for Fine-Grained and In-Depth Evaluation of MLLMs in Medical Imaging
items.abstract: The paper introduces **MedRCube**, a novel multidimensional framework for fine-grained and in-depth evaluation of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) in medical imaging, addressing the limitations of existing coarse metrics. This framework, built via a systematic construction pipeline, allows for rigorous assessment of reasoning mechanisms and clinical alignment. Benchmarking 33 models, MedRCube reveals critical insights, including a significant link between shortcut learning and diagnostic performance, while identifying Lingshu-32B as a top performer.
items.figure_caption: Paradigm shift required for evaluating multimodal large language models in medical imaging.

items.title: CLIP Architecture for Abdominal CT Image-Text Alignment and Zero-Shot Learning: Investigating Batch Composition and Data Scaling
items.abstract: This paper adapts the CLIP architecture, using a dual-encoder trained with symmetric InfoNCE loss, to align 3D abdominal CT scans with radiology reports for zero-shot diagnosis. The core contribution is investigating how training batch composition (normal-to-abnormal ratios) and data scaling affect the learned representations. They find that controlled balanced sampling degrades performance compared to the naturally unbalanced dataset, while performance scales sub-linearly with data reduction.
items.figure_caption: Zero-shot macro F1 by batch composition strategy. The baseline with shuffled sampling (no class balancing) outperforms all three balanced-sampling ratios by 2.4–2.8 percentage points. Within the balanced family, higher normal proportion yields marginally better results (75:25 > > 50:50 > > 25:75).

items.title: MApLe: Multi-instance Alignment of Diagnostic Reports and Large Medical Images
items.abstract: MApLe is a multi-instance vision-language alignment method designed to link specific diagnostic findings in medical reports to corresponding small regions in large medical images. It achieves this by disentangling anatomical and diagnostic concepts within the text embedding and using a patch-wise image encoder conditioned on anatomical structures. This approach enables precise localization of subtle pathological observations described in the reports, overcoming limitations of standard vision-language models.
items.figure_caption: Multi-instance alignment of diagnostic reports and image volumes. (a) During training the report is decomposed into sentences, each describing a finding, and its corresponding class (e.g., present or not). Anatomical regions in images are segmented, and patches are extracted. A finding specific multi-instance alignment of sentence- and image representations maps textual finding classes and associated image patches into a joint embedding space. (b) During test time, all patches of an anatomical region are first encoded and than pooled together using the corresponding finding network. The resulting profile describes the diagnostically relevant findings.

items.title: A Multi-Stage Optimization Pipeline for Bethesda Cell Detection in Pap Smear Cytology
items.abstract: This paper presents a multi-stage optimization pipeline for detecting Bethesda cells in Pap smear images, developed for the ISBI Riva Cytology Challenge. The core method combines an ensemble of YOLO and U-Net architectures for initial detection, followed by a refinement stage using overlap removal and a binary classifier. This framework achieved a strong second-place finish in the competition with a mAP50-95 score of 0.5909.

items.title: A Multimodal Clinically Informed Coarse-to-Fine Framework for Longitudinal CT Registration in Proton Therapy
items.abstract: This paper introduces a clinically scalable, coarse-to-fine framework for longitudinal CT registration in proton therapy, addressing the speed and clinical relevance limitations of existing methods. The model uses dual CNN encoders and a transformer decoder to progressively refine deformation fields, crucially incorporating multimodal clinical priors like contours, dose, and planning text to guide the registration process. This integration of clinical context enhances accuracy and robustness for time-sensitive adaptive radiotherapy workflows.

items.title: DiffMagicFace: Identity Consistent Facial Editing of Real Videos
items.abstract: DiffMagicFace is a novel video editing framework that uses two concurrently operating, fine-tuned diffusion models to achieve text-conditioned facial video editing. Its core contribution is ensuring strong identity preservation and cross-frame consistency in the edited video, achieved partly through a specially constructed dataset of varied facial perspectives for each subject. This method delivers high-quality results without relying on pre-existing video datasets.
items.figure_caption: Identity consistent face video editing. Our method achieves face video editing on a specific subject (e.g., half-frame glasses, or fat facial effect) using a text prompt, while preserving the identity of the source video and maintaining high consistency among frames.

items.title: Efficient Multi-View 3D Object Detection by Dynamic Token Selection and Fine-Tuning
items.abstract: This paper proposes a method to efficiently accelerate multi-view 3D object detection using Vision Transformers (ViTs) by introducing **dynamic layer-wise token selection** within the backbone, overcoming the fixed ratios of previous work. Additionally, it employs a **parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategy** instead of full retraining. This combination significantly improves computational efficiency during both training and inference for 3D object detection tasks.

items.title: Enhanced Text-to-Image Generation by Fine-grained Multimodal Reasoning
items.abstract: This paper introduces **Fine-grained Multimodal Reasoning (FiMR)**, a framework that enhances text-to-image generation by leveraging the reasoning capabilities of unified MLLMs. FiMR decomposes input prompts into minimal semantic units (entities/attributes) and uses decomposed Visual Question Answering (VQA) to generate explicit, fine-grained feedback. This feedback enables targeted, localized refinements, overcoming the limitations of existing methods that rely only on holistic alignment judgments.
items.figure_caption: An example of the text-to-image reasoning process of Janus-Pro-R1.

items.title: Feed-Forward 3D Scene Modeling: A Problem-Driven Perspective
items.abstract: This paper surveys feed-forward methods for 3D scene modeling, which aim to reconstruct 3D representations directly from 2D inputs in a single forward pass, overcoming the limitations of slow optimization or category-specific training. The core contribution is a problem-driven perspective that unifies these diverse approaches by identifying shared high-level architectural patterns, such as feature extraction, multi-view fusion, and geometry-aware decoders, across various output representations. This framework helps understand the current landscape and guides future research toward more generalizable and efficient 3D reconstruction.
items.figure_caption: Outline of the survey. The paper begins with an overview of core 3D representations (NeRF [ 1 ] , 3D Gaussian Splatting [ 2 ] , Pointmap [ 3 ] , and others [ 4 , 5 , 6 , 7 , 8 ] ) and how feed-forward networks generate them. It then analyzes methods along five challenge-driven axes: feature enhancement, geometry awareness, model efficiency, data or visual augmentation, and temporal modeling, followed by reviews of datasets and benchmarks, practical applications ( e.g. , autonomous driving and robotics). Finally, based on a statistical summary of the surveyed works, we outline recent trends and discuss future directions for feed-forward 3D scene modeling.

items.title: Free Geometry: Refining 3D Reconstruction from Longer Versions of Itself
items.abstract: This paper introduces **Free Geometry**, a novel framework that allows efficient, feed-forward 3D reconstruction models to self-refine their output during testing without needing 3D ground truth. The core method leverages the increased reliability of reconstructions generated from longer input sequences by enforcing cross-view feature consistency between full and partially masked observations. This self-supervision enables fast, lightweight recalibration (via LoRA updates) to correct errors arising from ambiguities like occlusion.
items.figure_caption: Free Geometry enables feed-forward 3D reconstruction models to self-evolve at test time without any 3D ground truth and generalize on models and datasets.

items.title: Indexing Multimodal Language Models for Large-scale Image Retrieval
items.abstract: This paper proposes using pre-trained Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) as training-free similarity estimators for instance-level image retrieval. The core method involves prompting the MLLM with paired images and converting next-token probabilities into similarity scores for zero-shot re-ranking. This approach leverages the MLLMs' inherent visual knowledge without fine-tuning, demonstrating superior robustness compared to specialized re-rankers in large-scale retrieval pipelines.

items.title: Med-CAM: Minimal Evidence for Explaining Medical Decision Making
items.abstract: Med-CAM introduces a novel framework to generate **minimal and sharp segmentation masks** as evidence for medical AI decisions, addressing the opacity of black-box models. It trains a dedicated segmentation network to highlight only the **critical, minimal evidence** necessary for the model's diagnosis. This approach provides explanations with superior spatial awareness compared to prior methods, making them more faithful and interpretable for clinicians.
items.figure_caption: Overview of the Med-CAM framework. Given an input medical image x x , a lightweight U-Net produces a binary mask m m , yielding the masked explanation e = m ⊙ x e=m\( \odot \) x . The mask highlights the minimal diagnostic evidence required to preserve the model’s decision.

items.title: One Token per Highly Selective Frame: Towards Extreme Compression for Long Video Understanding
items.abstract: This paper proposes **LP-Comp (Learnable and Progressive Compression)** to address the token overload in long video understanding by aiming for one token per frame at the final LLM layer. Instead of heuristic methods, LP-Comp uses learnable, progressive modules for token-level compression, allowing the VLM to process significantly more frames with improved performance. The method is further enhanced by investigating frame-level compression based on internal LLM attention scores.
items.figure_caption: Left: We present XComp that explores using the LLM layers to progressively compress the video tokens towards the extreme of one token per frame . Right: Such capabilities enable the model to better improve itself with denser input frames without significantly increasing video tokens.

items.title: Reconstruction of a 3D wireframe from a single line drawing via generative depth estimation
items.abstract: This paper proposes a novel generative method to reconstruct 3D wireframes from single 2D line drawings by framing it as a conditional dense depth estimation task. It utilizes a Latent Diffusion Model conditioned via a ControlNet-style framework to resolve projection ambiguities. The contribution lies in enabling flexible 3D reconstruction beyond rigid primitives, supporting iterative workflows through a BFS masking strategy for partial depth simulation.
items.figure_caption: Line drawing reconstruction graphic. a) A wireframe sketch, in this case a cube, presents numerous “possible” reconstructions. b) Actual 2D input line drawing, predicted depth map, and 3D reconstruction output.

items.title: Reward Design for Physical Reasoning in Vision-Language Models
items.abstract: This paper investigates how different reward designs impact the physical reasoning capabilities of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) using a Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) framework. The core method involves a systematic ablation study comparing four reward signals, ranging from simple format compliance to a novel reward derived from the model's internal attention weights. The contribution is a comprehensive analysis demonstrating the effect of reward richness on VLM performance across diverse physics reasoning tasks.
items.figure_caption: Reward design spectrum for GRPO-based VLM training on physical reasoning. Given a PhyX image-question pair, the model generates structured outputs and is trained with GRPO. We compare four rewards: (R1) format, (R2) accuracy, (R3) rubric (correctness, principle, unit), and (R4) attention-based visual grounding.

items.title: ROSE: Retrieval-Oriented Segmentation Enhancement
items.abstract: The paper introduces the Novel Emerging Segmentation Task (NEST) to address the challenge of segmenting new or emerging entities using multimodal large language models (MLLMs). The core method is **ROSE (Retrieval-Oriented Segmentation Enhancement)**, a plug-and-play framework that augments existing MLLM segmenters by integrating an Internet Retrieval-Augmented Generation module. This allows the model to access up-to-date external knowledge to accurately segment entities beyond its initial training data.

items.title: SLQ: Bridging Modalities via Shared Latent Queries for Retrieval with Frozen MLLMs
items.abstract: SLQ proposes an efficient method to adapt frozen Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for retrieval without invasive fine-tuning. It introduces a small set of Shared Latent Queries appended to token sequences, which use the MLLM's native attention to generate compact, unified embeddings for both text and images. This approach elicits existing knowledge for retrieval while preserving the MLLM's core reasoning capabilities.

items.title: Towards Unconstrained Human-Object Interaction
items.abstract: This paper introduces the **Unconstrained Human-Object Interaction (U-HOI)** task, removing the need for a predefined interaction vocabulary during training and inference. The core method leverages **Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs)** to recognize interactions from free-form text, employing a pipeline that converts the MLLM's textual output into structured interaction graphs. This work significantly contributes by enabling flexible, open-vocabulary HOI detection in real-world scenarios.
items.figure_caption: Comparison between traditional HOI settings and our U-HOI. Unlike prior art, U-HOI does not require training and eliminates the need for a predefined list of interactions. We tackle U-HOI using off-the-shelf Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), processing their free-form outputs and applying a post-generation refinement that extracts HOI triplets, all without being constrained by a fixed vocabulary.

items.title: Training-Free Semantic Multi-Object Tracking with Vision-Language Models
items.abstract: This paper introduces TF-SMOT, a **training-free** pipeline for Semantic Multi-Object Tracking (SMOT) that leverages existing pretrained Vision-Language Models (VLMs). The core method **composes** components like SAM2 for tracking and InternVideo2.5 for captioning, using LLM-based semantic retrieval to generate human-interpretable outputs like summaries and interaction labels **without requiring end-to-end training**. This approach significantly improves adaptability by decoupling tracking progress from expensive, task-specific supervision.
items.figure_caption: Comparison of training-based and training-free Semantic Multi-Object Tracking (SMOT) paradigms. Top: Traditional SMOT systems like SMOTer [ 16 ] require task-specific end-to-end training on annotated datasets, coupling tracking and semantic prediction to supervised learning. Bottom: our TF-SMOT operates entirely at inference time by composing frozen, pretrained components to produce tracking outputs together with video summaries, instance-level captions, and directed interactions, without using SMOT training data.

items.title: What Are We Really Measuring? Rethinking Dataset Bias in Web-Scale Natural Image Collections via Unsupervised Semantic Clustering
items.abstract: This paper challenges the standard method of measuring dataset bias by training classifiers, arguing that high accuracy often stems from resolution-based artifacts rather than true semantic differences. The authors demonstrate that these structural fingerprints, arising from native image resolutions and resizing, create robust, dataset-specific signatures that persist despite common augmentations. Their core contribution is showing that current bias metrics are frequently measuring these non-semantic artifacts, not meaningful semantic divergence.
items.figure_caption: The ”Name That Dataset” game [ 36 ] aware of resolution distributions. These images are sampled from the YFCC and CC datasets. We maintain their relative sizes. Additionally, we provide a plot showing the resolution distribution of the training samples from each dataset. Can you guess which dataset each image is from? Once resolution effects are considered, dataset separability becomes significantly more apparent. (Answer: YFCC: 3, 5, 6, 8, 9; CC: 1, 2, 4, 7)

items.title: Why MLLMs Struggle to Determine Object Orientations
items.abstract: This paper investigates why Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) fail at 2D object orientation tasks by testing the hypothesis that visual encoders (like CLIP/SigLIP) lack geometric reasoning capabilities. The core method involves training linear regressors on encoder features (from models like LLaVA and Qwen) to predict object orientation, using both full images and rotated foreground patches. The contribution is the empirical finding that, contrary to the hypothesis, orientation information *is* preserved in the encoder embeddings, suggesting the failure lies elsewhere in the MLLM architecture.
items.figure_caption: Set of images - Sets A and B are used for experiments with LLaVA-OV and Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, and set C is used for LLaVA1.5 and LLaVA1.6. Since LLaVA-OV and Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct can be prompted with 2 images, real images were used. But since LLaVA1.5 and 1.6 can be prompted with only 1 image, synthetic images with reduced artifacts were used to improve the prediction accuracy. [A: Whole Images] : (a) dog (b) train (c) lizard (d) beach (e) indoor (f) fish. [B: In-Place Rotated Objects] : (a) koala on beach (b) vase on indoor (c) vase and toaster on indoor. [C: Blended Images with foreground on background] : (a) dog on beach (b) lizard on fish (c) train on indoor environment, with foreground scales 1, 2 and 3 from left to right.

items.title: Context Sensitivity Improves Human-Machine Visual Alignment
items.abstract: This paper introduces a method for context-sensitive similarity computation using neural network embeddings, aiming to bridge the gap between fixed machine representations and human's context-adaptive processing. By incorporating an anchor image as simultaneous context in a triplet odd-one-out task, the approach achieves up to a 15% improvement in accuracy over context-insensitive models. This demonstrates that modeling context significantly enhances visual alignment, regardless of the underlying vision foundation model used.
items.figure_caption: Qualitative comparison of context-insensitive and context-sensitive models. (a) Example triplet, where the human odd-one-out choice was made anchored in a given context. The context-sensitive model matches the human choice, while the context-insensitive model does not, reflecting the ambiguity of the triplet without context. (b) Representational similarity matrices (RSMs) over a reference image set, showing how the context-sensitive model modulates similarities given the context image in a. (c) PCA plot of the reference set and the three triplet images (same symbols as in a). The horse-cart image shifts to the animal cluster for the context-sensitive model.

items.title: Decoding the Delta: Unifying Remote Sensing Change Detection and Understanding with Multimodal Large Language Models
items.abstract: This paper introduces **Delta-LLaVA**, a novel Multimodal Large Language Model framework designed to overcome the "temporal blindness" in remote sensing change detection. The core method employs a **Change-Enhanced Attention module** and a **Change-SEG module** to explicitly model and integrate multi-temporal visual differences with language understanding. The contribution is a unified approach for precise spatial grounding and cognitive-level change interpretation, validated by the new **Delta-QA benchmark**.
items.figure_caption: Figure 1 . Our work primarily addresses the unified task of multi-temporal remote sensing change detection and understanding. The proposed Delta-QA dataset encompasses various QA task types and interaction modalities.

items.title: Event-Adaptive State Transition and Gated Fusion for RGB-Event Object Tracking
items.abstract: This paper introduces MambaTrack, an RGB-Event object tracking framework that addresses static state transition issues in existing methods. Its core contribution is an **event-adaptive state transition mechanism** that dynamically adjusts the state transition matrix based on event stream density using a learnable scalar. Additionally, it employs a **Gated Projection Fusion (GPF) module** for robust cross-modal integration, generating adaptive gates from event density and RGB confidence.
items.figure_caption: Illustration of the proposed DSSM module with the event-adaptive state transition mechanism. Based on the standard State Space Model (SSM), our DSSM module incorporates an event-adaptive state transition mechanism (highlighted in red), which dynamically modulates the transition dynamics according to the event stream, enabling better temporal modeling under varying event sparsity.

items.title: Free Lunch for Unified Multimodal Models: Enhancing Generation via Reflective Rectification with Inherent Understanding
items.abstract: This paper introduces UniRect-CoT, a training-free framework that leverages the inherent, powerful understanding capabilities of Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) to improve their weaker generation performance. Inspired by human "Thinking-While-Drawing," the method treats the diffusion denoising process as visual reasoning, enabling the model to continuously reflect and rectify intermediate generation results using its existing knowledge. This "reflective rectification" unlocks a "free lunch" by activating dormant knowledge to enhance generative quality.
items.figure_caption: An illustration of the capability mismatch in UMMs . The UMM fails to generate “purple pizza” as instructed, but it correctly identifies the color as “red” and accurately describes the intended target in the understanding task.

items.title: Multi-Agent Object Detection Framework Based on Raspberry Pi YOLO Detector and Slack-Ollama Natural Language Interface
items.abstract: This paper proposes a multi-agent object detection framework implemented on resource-constrained Raspberry Pi hardware using a YOLO detector. Its core contribution is demonstrating a fast prototyping approach that leverages an LLM-based natural language interface (Slack/Ollama) for system control and communication, moving beyond traditional specialized interfaces.

items.title: POINTS-Seeker: Towards Training a Multimodal Agentic Search Model from Scratch
items.abstract: This paper introduces **POINTS-Seeker**, a novel approach to training a multimodal agentic search model entirely from scratch, rather than retrofitting existing LMMs. The core method involves an **Agentic Seeding** phase to establish foundational agentic behaviors, and the **V-Fold** compression scheme to overcome performance bottlenecks in long-horizon interactions by adaptively managing interaction history. This work contributes a new paradigm for building intrinsically agentic multimodal search systems.
items.figure_caption: Overview of POINTS-Seeker. Our model adaptively interacts with external tools to solve complex, multi-hop VQA tasks. By leveraging V-Fold compression, stale history is rendered into compact visual tokens, effectively bypassing long-context performance degradation while achieving superior efficiency and reasoning fidelity.

items.title: Remote Sensing Image Super-Resolution for Imbalanced Textures: A Texture-Aware Diffusion Framework
items.abstract: This paper introduces TexADiff, a novel diffusion framework specifically designed for remote sensing image super-resolution (RSISR) to overcome challenges posed by imbalanced textures. The core method involves estimating a Relative Texture Density Map (RTDM) which is then used to explicitly condition the diffusion process, modulate the loss function to prioritize texture-rich areas, and dynamically adapt the sampling schedule. This texture-aware conditioning significantly improves the model's spatial perception and detail synthesis in complex remote sensing imagery.
items.figure_caption: Our method produces faithful, fine-grained details in the texture-rich ship regions, while avoiding stripe artifacts in the texture-sparse water regions.

items.title: Seek-and-Solve: Benchmarking MLLMs for Visual Clue-Driven Reasoning in Daily Scenarios
items.abstract: This paper introduces **DailyClue**, a novel benchmark designed to evaluate Multimodal Large Language Models' (MLLMs) ability for **visual clue-driven reasoning** in authentic daily scenarios. The core method involves creating challenging questions grounded in real-world activities that force models to actively identify and leverage specific visual clues for complex reasoning, rather than relying on simple perception or pre-existing knowledge. This contributes a crucial evaluation tool that specifically targets and measures the critical reasoning capabilities often neglected by existing benchmarks.
items.figure_caption: Overview of DailyClue. The left panel shows the hierarchical distribution (labels abbreviated for clarity), while the right panels detail the full taxonomy, data sources, and question types.

items.title: Why Multimodal In-Context Learning Lags Behind? Unveiling the Inner Mechanisms and Bottlenecks
items.abstract: This paper systematically analyzes why multimodal in-context learning (ICL) underperforms text-only ICL with few-shot examples. The authors decompose multimodal ICL into task mapping construction and transfer, revealing that current models suffer from a lack of reasoning-level alignment between visual and textual representations and struggle to reliably transfer learned task mappings to new queries. This work identifies the cross-modal alignment and transfer process as the key bottleneck limiting multimodal ICL performance.
items.figure_caption: ( a–b ) Illustration of constructed outlier detection task Chen et al. ( 2025a ) , where (a) shows the zero-shot setup and (b) shows the few-shot setup. In the few-shot scenario (2-shot), the model must infer from the demonstrations whether the query should be solved based on shape or color, and then apply this inferred rule to identify the outlier item in either the image or the sentence. ( c ) Performance comparison of Qwen2.5-VL-7B and Gemma-3-12B, showing that multimodal ICL suffers a clear degradation under the few-shot setting (4-shot) relative to text-only ICL. ( d ) Illustration of the multimodal ICL pipeline, including task mapping construction from demonstrations and task mapping transfer to the query.

items.title: MAny: Merge Anything for Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning
items.abstract: The paper introduces **MAny** (Merge Anything), a framework to address catastrophic forgetting in Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning (MCIT) by tackling dual forgetting in both perception and reasoning spaces. MAny achieves this through **Cross-modal Projection Merging (CPM)** to recover visual alignment via prototype guidance, and **Low-rank Parameter Merging (LPM)** to mitigate interference among task-specific parameters. This dual-merging approach effectively preserves past knowledge while adapting to new multimodal tasks.
items.figure_caption: Performance diagnosis on UCIT benchmark for LLaVA-1.5-7B and InternVL-Chat-7B during sequential LoRA fine-tuning. Tasks follow a clockwise sequence from Image-R.

items.title: Out of Context: Reliability in Multimodal Anomaly Detection Requires Contextual Inference
items.abstract: This paper addresses the unreliability of standard anomaly detection methods that assume a single, unconditional model of "normal" behavior. The core method introduces **contextual inference** to distinguish genuine anomalies from variations in operating conditions, recognizing that an observation's normalcy is context-dependent. This approach leverages multimodal data to build a more robust and context-aware reference model, significantly improving assessment reliability in dynamic environments.

items.title: Inclusive Kitchen Design for Older Adults: Generative AI Visualizations to Support Mild Cognitive Impairment
items.abstract: This paper introduces a Generative AI system, leveraging Stable Diffusion with DreamBooth and ControlNet, to automatically transform standard kitchen photos into designs optimized for older adults with Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI). The system applies Home Design Guidelines to create visualizations featuring open layouts and better accessibility features. The core contribution is demonstrating that these AI-generated, cognitively-friendly kitchen visualizations are strongly preferred by users, offering a scalable design aid where professional help is limited.

items.title: Jump-Start Reinforcement Learning with Vision-Language-Action Regularization
items.abstract: This paper introduces **Vision-Language-Action Jump-Starting (VLAJS)** to accelerate Reinforcement Learning (RL) for complex manipulation tasks. VLAJS uses a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model to provide transient, high-level action suggestions that regularize and guide the early exploration of an on-policy RL agent (PPO). This method effectively bridges the general reasoning of VLA models with the precise, high-frequency control of RL, significantly improving learning efficiency and exploration.

items.title: Lazy or Efficient? Towards Accessible Eye-Tracking Event Detection Using LLMs
items.abstract: This paper introduces an accessible, code-free pipeline utilizing Large Language Models (LLMs) for eye-tracking event detection. The system translates natural language instructions into executable routines for data inspection, cleaning, and applying standard detectors to label fixations and saccades. This significantly lowers the barrier to entry for gaze analysis by automating complex preprocessing and parameterization steps.
items.figure_caption: Figure 1. This figure compares our method with the traditional manual approach. In particular, the LLM-assisted workflow on the right illustrates our pipeline: (1) starting from raw data, the LLM automatically identifies relevant fields, assesses data quality characteristics, and generates an easy-to-understand summary; (2) users specify their requirements in natural language, and the LLM produces executable data-cleaning code together with parameterized implementations of selected event detectors (e.g., I-VT, I-DT); (3) the cleaned data is then passed to a detector set composed of multiple event-labeling algorithms; and (4) the results and explanatory reports are returned, allowing users to iteratively refine the code and parameters by editing prompts and rerunning the detection process.

items.title: UMI-3D: Extending Universal Manipulation Interface from Vision-Limited to 3D Spatial Perception
items.abstract: UMI-3D extends the wrist-mounted Universal Manipulation Interface (UMI) by integrating a low-cost LiDAR sensor to overcome the visual limitations of its predecessor. This enables robust, LiDAR-centric SLAM for accurate metric-scale pose estimation in challenging real-world manipulation scenarios. The core contribution is a synchronized multimodal sensing pipeline that produces consistent 3D representations for reliable data collection, significantly improving the quality of demonstrations for visuomotor policies.

items.title: Enhancing Reinforcement Learning for Radiology Report Generation with Evidence-aware Rewards and Self-correcting Preference Learning
items.abstract: This paper introduces **ESC-RL**, a novel reinforcement learning framework for radiology report generation. It employs a **Group-wise Evidence-aware Alignment Reward (GEAR)** to provide fine-grained, evidence-grounded feedback, improving clinical faithfulness. Furthermore, it utilizes **Self-correcting Preference Learning (SPL)** to automatically build a reliable preference dataset and refine reports using an LLM, enabling self-improvement aligned with clinical standards.
items.figure_caption: Overview of the proposed Evidence-aware Self-Correcting Reinforcement Learning (ESC-RL) framework.

items.title: Multi-Task LLM with LoRA Fine-Tuning for Automated Cancer Staging and Biomarker Extraction
items.abstract: This paper presents a parameter-efficient, multi-task framework using LoRA fine-tuning on Llama-3-8B-Instruct to automate the extraction of cancer staging (TNM), grade, and biomarkers from unstructured pathology reports. By employing parallel classification heads instead of purely generative methods, the model ensures schema adherence and effectively handles complex, heterogeneous report formats. The core contribution is achieving high accuracy (Macro F1 of 0.976) for structured data extraction while mitigating the computational cost and hallucination risks associated with large, fully fine-tuned LLMs.
items.figure_caption: Detailed architecture of the proposed Multi-Task LLM framework. (a) Data Preprocessing: Raw pathology reports undergo native digital text extraction, de-identification, and human-verified ground truth generation before tokenization. (b) Data Splitting: The dataset is partitioned into training (80%), validation (10%), and testing (10%) sets to support model development and unbiased evaluation. (c) Model Architecture: A shared Llama-3 encoder (fine-tuned via LoRA) extracts high-dimensional semantic features from the terminal hidden state, which are simultaneously projected into parallel task-specific classification heads (T, N, M, Grade, ER, PR, HER2). (d) Optimization Loop: The training process minimizes a joint cross-entropy loss, updating only the low-rank adapters and head weights while keeping the heavy LLM backbone frozen. (e) Evaluation: The system produces a structured output vector containing the predicted class for each clinical variable, providing a comprehensive TNM and biomarker profile.

items.title: MOONSHOT : A Framework for Multi-Objective Pruning of Vision and Large Language Models
items.abstract: MOONSHOT is a framework that enhances post-training, one-shot model pruning by transforming single-objective methods into a multi-objective optimization. It jointly minimizes both the layer-wise reconstruction error and the second-order Taylor approximation of the training loss, addressing the inconsistency of using either objective alone. This wrapper approach provides a general and scalable way to improve pruning effectiveness across various model architectures and sparsity levels.

items.title: Text-Attributed Knowledge Graph Enrichment with Large Language Models for Medical Concept Representation
items.abstract: This paper introduces **CoMed**, an LLM-empowered framework to enrich medical knowledge graphs (KGs) for better medical concept representation. CoMed addresses limitations in existing KGs by integrating statistically derived, clinically relevant cross-type dependencies from EHRs with rich semantic information extracted from clinical text using Large Language Models. This dual integration results in higher-quality concept embeddings crucial for downstream clinical prediction tasks.
items.figure_caption: Overview of CoMed . (1) Extract intra-visit co-occurrence and next-visit transition evidence from EHRs, and retain well-supported code pairs. (2) Use type-constrained, evidence-conditioned LLM prompting to assign directed relation types (with confidence and rationales), thereby constructing a heterogeneous KG. (3) Enrich nodes with LLM-generated concept descriptions and edges with relation metadata. (4) Jointly train a LoRA-tuned LLaMA encoder and a relation-aware GNN to learn medical concept embeddings for downstream prediction.

items.title: Dataset-Level Metrics Attenuate Non-Determinism: A Fine-Grained Non-Determinism Evaluation in Diffusion Language Models
items.abstract: This paper argues that existing dataset-level metrics mask the true non-deterministic behavior of Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) by averaging out sample-level variations across runs. The core contribution is a fine-grained evaluation method that analyzes prediction differences at the *sample level* across various inference factors (like guidance scale) to accurately characterize DLM instability. This reveals that similar aggregate performance can hide substantially different error patterns for individual inputs.

items.title: Reward Hacking in the Era of Large Models: Mechanisms, Emergent Misalignment, Challenges
items.abstract: This paper surveys the mechanisms and emergent risks of **reward hacking** in LLMs and MLLMs trained via alignment techniques like RLHF. The core contribution is proposing the **Proxy Compression Hypothesis (PCH)** as a unifying framework to explain how models exploit imperfect reward signals, leading to misalignment behaviors like verbosity and sycophancy.
items.figure_caption: The illusion of alignment: Manifestations of reward hacking across diverse model families. When guided by imperfect proxy evaluators (Reward Models), models often discover strategies that maximize proxy scores (green boxes) while actively bypassing the true task intent (red boxes). (1) Large Language Models: Exploiting preference proxies through sycophancy and factual compromise. (2) Multimodal Language Models: Bypassing genuine visual grounding via metric-specific shortcuts , such as artificially inflating bounding boxes. (3) Visual Generative Models: Suffering structural degradation (e.g., generating a two-headed dog) when over-optimizing for specific semantic features. (4) Agentic Models: Engaging in environment-level exploitation by directly tampering with oversight mechanisms and static unit tests.

items.title: Robust Ultra Low-Bit Post-Training Quantization via Stable Diagonal Curvature Estimate
items.abstract: This paper introduces DASH-Q, a robust Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) framework designed for ultra low-bit quantization of Large Language Models (LLMs). It addresses the instability of traditional Hessian-based methods by employing a diagonal Hessian approximation combined with iterative weighted least squares to filter out noise from limited calibration data. DASH-Q significantly improves zero-shot accuracy in the ultra low-bit regime, outperforming existing baselines by up to 14.01%.
items.figure_caption: Figure 1. (a) Relative ℓ 1 \( \ell_{1} \) error of the Hessian estimate computed from n n calibration samples against a reference with n = 4096 n=4096 samples, (b) Relative ℓ 1 \( \ell_{1} \) error between two independent 128-sample sets over 100 trials. Results are measured at the 10th transformer layer of Llama-2-7B ( s ​ e ​ q = 2048 seq=2048 ).

items.title: Universality of Gaussian-Mixture Reverse Kernels in Conditional Diffusion
items.abstract: This paper proves that conditional diffusion models using reverse kernels defined by finite Gaussian mixtures with ReLU networks can approximate target distributions arbitrarily well in context-averaged conditional KL divergence. This is achieved by decomposing the path-space error and showing that each step reduces to a static conditional density approximation problem, solvable via existing Gaussian-mixture theory and ReLU bounds. The core contribution is establishing the universal approximation capability of this specific class of neural reverse kernels under conditional KL divergence.

arxiv.title: ADP-DiT: Text-Guided Diffusion Transformer for Brain Image Generation in Alzheimer's Disease Progression
arxiv.abstract: ADP-DiT is a novel Diffusion Transformer designed for generating subject-specific, longitudinal Alzheimer's Disease (AD) brain MRI scans. It achieves clinically interpretable control by encoding follow-up time intervals and multi-domain patient metadata (demographics, diagnosis, neuropsychology) into a natural-language prompt. This prompt is processed by dual text encoders (OpenCLIP and T5) to guide the image synthesis, allowing for time-specific progression modeling beyond simple diagnostic stages.
arxiv.figure_caption: The overview of the proposed ADP-DiT framework. The model conditions on multiple text-encoders text embeddings and structured metadata to guide the denoising process in the latent space.

arxiv.title: CausalDisenSeg: A Causality-Guided Disentanglement Framework with Counterfactual Reasoning for Robust Brain Tumor Segmentation Under Missing Modalities
arxiv.abstract: CausalDisenSeg addresses robust brain tumor segmentation under missing MRI modalities by employing a Structural Causal Model (SCM) framework. The core method uses causality-guided disentanglement to explicitly separate the anatomical Causal Factor from the modality-dependent Bias Factor using a CVAE with an HSIC constraint. This allows the model to learn robust segmentations by relying only on the causally relevant features, even when some modalities are absent.

arxiv.title: MedRCube: A Multidimensional Framework for Fine-Grained and In-Depth Evaluation of MLLMs in Medical Imaging
arxiv.abstract: The paper introduces **MedRCube**, a novel multidimensional framework for fine-grained and in-depth evaluation of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) in medical imaging, addressing the limitations of existing coarse metrics. This framework, built via a systematic construction pipeline, allows for rigorous assessment of reasoning mechanisms and clinical alignment. Benchmarking 33 models, MedRCube reveals critical insights, including a significant link between shortcut learning and diagnostic performance, while identifying Lingshu-32B as a top performer.
arxiv.figure_caption: Paradigm shift required for evaluating multimodal large language models in medical imaging.

arxiv.title: CLIP Architecture for Abdominal CT Image-Text Alignment and Zero-Shot Learning: Investigating Batch Composition and Data Scaling
arxiv.abstract: This paper adapts the CLIP architecture, using a dual-encoder trained with symmetric InfoNCE loss, to align 3D abdominal CT scans with radiology reports for zero-shot diagnosis. The core contribution is investigating how training batch composition (normal-to-abnormal ratios) and data scaling affect the learned representations. They find that controlled balanced sampling degrades performance compared to the naturally unbalanced dataset, while performance scales sub-linearly with data reduction.
arxiv.figure_caption: Zero-shot macro F1 by batch composition strategy. The baseline with shuffled sampling (no class balancing) outperforms all three balanced-sampling ratios by 2.4–2.8 percentage points. Within the balanced family, higher normal proportion yields marginally better results (75:25 > > 50:50 > > 25:75).

arxiv.title: MApLe: Multi-instance Alignment of Diagnostic Reports and Large Medical Images
arxiv.abstract: MApLe is a multi-instance vision-language alignment method designed to link specific diagnostic findings in medical reports to corresponding small regions in large medical images. It achieves this by disentangling anatomical and diagnostic concepts within the text embedding and using a patch-wise image encoder conditioned on anatomical structures. This approach enables precise localization of subtle pathological observations described in the reports, overcoming limitations of standard vision-language models.
arxiv.figure_caption: Multi-instance alignment of diagnostic reports and image volumes. (a) During training the report is decomposed into sentences, each describing a finding, and its corresponding class (e.g., present or not). Anatomical regions in images are segmented, and patches are extracted. A finding specific multi-instance alignment of sentence- and image representations maps textual finding classes and associated image patches into a joint embedding space. (b) During test time, all patches of an anatomical region are first encoded and than pooled together using the corresponding finding network. The resulting profile describes the diagnostically relevant findings.

arxiv.title: A Multi-Stage Optimization Pipeline for Bethesda Cell Detection in Pap Smear Cytology
arxiv.abstract: This paper presents a multi-stage optimization pipeline for detecting Bethesda cells in Pap smear images, developed for the ISBI Riva Cytology Challenge. The core method combines an ensemble of YOLO and U-Net architectures for initial detection, followed by a refinement stage using overlap removal and a binary classifier. This framework achieved a strong second-place finish in the competition with a mAP50-95 score of 0.5909.

arxiv.title: A Multimodal Clinically Informed Coarse-to-Fine Framework for Longitudinal CT Registration in Proton Therapy
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces a clinically scalable, coarse-to-fine framework for longitudinal CT registration in proton therapy, addressing the speed and clinical relevance limitations of existing methods. The model uses dual CNN encoders and a transformer decoder to progressively refine deformation fields, crucially incorporating multimodal clinical priors like contours, dose, and planning text to guide the registration process. This integration of clinical context enhances accuracy and robustness for time-sensitive adaptive radiotherapy workflows.

arxiv.title: DiffMagicFace: Identity Consistent Facial Editing of Real Videos
arxiv.abstract: DiffMagicFace is a novel video editing framework that uses two concurrently operating, fine-tuned diffusion models to achieve text-conditioned facial video editing. Its core contribution is ensuring strong identity preservation and cross-frame consistency in the edited video, achieved partly through a specially constructed dataset of varied facial perspectives for each subject. This method delivers high-quality results without relying on pre-existing video datasets.
arxiv.figure_caption: Identity consistent face video editing. Our method achieves face video editing on a specific subject (e.g., half-frame glasses, or fat facial effect) using a text prompt, while preserving the identity of the source video and maintaining high consistency among frames.

arxiv.title: Efficient Multi-View 3D Object Detection by Dynamic Token Selection and Fine-Tuning
arxiv.abstract: This paper proposes a method to efficiently accelerate multi-view 3D object detection using Vision Transformers (ViTs) by introducing **dynamic layer-wise token selection** within the backbone, overcoming the fixed ratios of previous work. Additionally, it employs a **parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategy** instead of full retraining. This combination significantly improves computational efficiency during both training and inference for 3D object detection tasks.

arxiv.title: Enhanced Text-to-Image Generation by Fine-grained Multimodal Reasoning
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces **Fine-grained Multimodal Reasoning (FiMR)**, a framework that enhances text-to-image generation by leveraging the reasoning capabilities of unified MLLMs. FiMR decomposes input prompts into minimal semantic units (entities/attributes) and uses decomposed Visual Question Answering (VQA) to generate explicit, fine-grained feedback. This feedback enables targeted, localized refinements, overcoming the limitations of existing methods that rely only on holistic alignment judgments.
arxiv.figure_caption: An example of the text-to-image reasoning process of Janus-Pro-R1.

arxiv.title: Feed-Forward 3D Scene Modeling: A Problem-Driven Perspective
arxiv.abstract: This paper surveys feed-forward methods for 3D scene modeling, which aim to reconstruct 3D representations directly from 2D inputs in a single forward pass, overcoming the limitations of slow optimization or category-specific training. The core contribution is a problem-driven perspective that unifies these diverse approaches by identifying shared high-level architectural patterns, such as feature extraction, multi-view fusion, and geometry-aware decoders, across various output representations. This framework helps understand the current landscape and guides future research toward more generalizable and efficient 3D reconstruction.
arxiv.figure_caption: Outline of the survey. The paper begins with an overview of core 3D representations (NeRF [ 1 ] , 3D Gaussian Splatting [ 2 ] , Pointmap [ 3 ] , and others [ 4 , 5 , 6 , 7 , 8 ] ) and how feed-forward networks generate them. It then analyzes methods along five challenge-driven axes: feature enhancement, geometry awareness, model efficiency, data or visual augmentation, and temporal modeling, followed by reviews of datasets and benchmarks, practical applications ( e.g. , autonomous driving and robotics). Finally, based on a statistical summary of the surveyed works, we outline recent trends and discuss future directions for feed-forward 3D scene modeling.

arxiv.title: Free Geometry: Refining 3D Reconstruction from Longer Versions of Itself
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces **Free Geometry**, a novel framework that allows efficient, feed-forward 3D reconstruction models to self-refine their output during testing without needing 3D ground truth. The core method leverages the increased reliability of reconstructions generated from longer input sequences by enforcing cross-view feature consistency between full and partially masked observations. This self-supervision enables fast, lightweight recalibration (via LoRA updates) to correct errors arising from ambiguities like occlusion.
arxiv.figure_caption: Free Geometry enables feed-forward 3D reconstruction models to self-evolve at test time without any 3D ground truth and generalize on models and datasets.

arxiv.title: Indexing Multimodal Language Models for Large-scale Image Retrieval
arxiv.abstract: This paper proposes using pre-trained Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) as training-free similarity estimators for instance-level image retrieval. The core method involves prompting the MLLM with paired images and converting next-token probabilities into similarity scores for zero-shot re-ranking. This approach leverages the MLLMs' inherent visual knowledge without fine-tuning, demonstrating superior robustness compared to specialized re-rankers in large-scale retrieval pipelines.

arxiv.title: Med-CAM: Minimal Evidence for Explaining Medical Decision Making
arxiv.abstract: Med-CAM introduces a novel framework to generate **minimal and sharp segmentation masks** as evidence for medical AI decisions, addressing the opacity of black-box models. It trains a dedicated segmentation network to highlight only the **critical, minimal evidence** necessary for the model's diagnosis. This approach provides explanations with superior spatial awareness compared to prior methods, making them more faithful and interpretable for clinicians.
arxiv.figure_caption: Overview of the Med-CAM framework. Given an input medical image x x , a lightweight U-Net produces a binary mask m m , yielding the masked explanation e = m ⊙ x e=m\( \odot \) x . The mask highlights the minimal diagnostic evidence required to preserve the model’s decision.

arxiv.title: One Token per Highly Selective Frame: Towards Extreme Compression for Long Video Understanding
arxiv.abstract: This paper proposes **LP-Comp (Learnable and Progressive Compression)** to address the token overload in long video understanding by aiming for one token per frame at the final LLM layer. Instead of heuristic methods, LP-Comp uses learnable, progressive modules for token-level compression, allowing the VLM to process significantly more frames with improved performance. The method is further enhanced by investigating frame-level compression based on internal LLM attention scores.
arxiv.figure_caption: Left: We present XComp that explores using the LLM layers to progressively compress the video tokens towards the extreme of one token per frame . Right: Such capabilities enable the model to better improve itself with denser input frames without significantly increasing video tokens.

arxiv.title: Reconstruction of a 3D wireframe from a single line drawing via generative depth estimation
arxiv.abstract: This paper proposes a novel generative method to reconstruct 3D wireframes from single 2D line drawings by framing it as a conditional dense depth estimation task. It utilizes a Latent Diffusion Model conditioned via a ControlNet-style framework to resolve projection ambiguities. The contribution lies in enabling flexible 3D reconstruction beyond rigid primitives, supporting iterative workflows through a BFS masking strategy for partial depth simulation.
arxiv.figure_caption: Line drawing reconstruction graphic. a) A wireframe sketch, in this case a cube, presents numerous “possible” reconstructions. b) Actual 2D input line drawing, predicted depth map, and 3D reconstruction output.

arxiv.title: Reward Design for Physical Reasoning in Vision-Language Models
arxiv.abstract: This paper investigates how different reward designs impact the physical reasoning capabilities of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) using a Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) framework. The core method involves a systematic ablation study comparing four reward signals, ranging from simple format compliance to a novel reward derived from the model's internal attention weights. The contribution is a comprehensive analysis demonstrating the effect of reward richness on VLM performance across diverse physics reasoning tasks.
arxiv.figure_caption: Reward design spectrum for GRPO-based VLM training on physical reasoning. Given a PhyX image-question pair, the model generates structured outputs and is trained with GRPO. We compare four rewards: (R1) format, (R2) accuracy, (R3) rubric (correctness, principle, unit), and (R4) attention-based visual grounding.

arxiv.title: ROSE: Retrieval-Oriented Segmentation Enhancement
arxiv.abstract: The paper introduces the Novel Emerging Segmentation Task (NEST) to address the challenge of segmenting new or emerging entities using multimodal large language models (MLLMs). The core method is **ROSE (Retrieval-Oriented Segmentation Enhancement)**, a plug-and-play framework that augments existing MLLM segmenters by integrating an Internet Retrieval-Augmented Generation module. This allows the model to access up-to-date external knowledge to accurately segment entities beyond its initial training data.

arxiv.title: SLQ: Bridging Modalities via Shared Latent Queries for Retrieval with Frozen MLLMs
arxiv.abstract: SLQ proposes an efficient method to adapt frozen Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for retrieval without invasive fine-tuning. It introduces a small set of Shared Latent Queries appended to token sequences, which use the MLLM's native attention to generate compact, unified embeddings for both text and images. This approach elicits existing knowledge for retrieval while preserving the MLLM's core reasoning capabilities.

arxiv.title: Towards Unconstrained Human-Object Interaction
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces the **Unconstrained Human-Object Interaction (U-HOI)** task, removing the need for a predefined interaction vocabulary during training and inference. The core method leverages **Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs)** to recognize interactions from free-form text, employing a pipeline that converts the MLLM's textual output into structured interaction graphs. This work significantly contributes by enabling flexible, open-vocabulary HOI detection in real-world scenarios.
arxiv.figure_caption: Comparison between traditional HOI settings and our U-HOI. Unlike prior art, U-HOI does not require training and eliminates the need for a predefined list of interactions. We tackle U-HOI using off-the-shelf Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), processing their free-form outputs and applying a post-generation refinement that extracts HOI triplets, all without being constrained by a fixed vocabulary.

arxiv.title: Training-Free Semantic Multi-Object Tracking with Vision-Language Models
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces TF-SMOT, a **training-free** pipeline for Semantic Multi-Object Tracking (SMOT) that leverages existing pretrained Vision-Language Models (VLMs). The core method **composes** components like SAM2 for tracking and InternVideo2.5 for captioning, using LLM-based semantic retrieval to generate human-interpretable outputs like summaries and interaction labels **without requiring end-to-end training**. This approach significantly improves adaptability by decoupling tracking progress from expensive, task-specific supervision.
arxiv.figure_caption: Comparison of training-based and training-free Semantic Multi-Object Tracking (SMOT) paradigms. Top: Traditional SMOT systems like SMOTer [ 16 ] require task-specific end-to-end training on annotated datasets, coupling tracking and semantic prediction to supervised learning. Bottom: our TF-SMOT operates entirely at inference time by composing frozen, pretrained components to produce tracking outputs together with video summaries, instance-level captions, and directed interactions, without using SMOT training data.

arxiv.title: What Are We Really Measuring? Rethinking Dataset Bias in Web-Scale Natural Image Collections via Unsupervised Semantic Clustering
arxiv.abstract: This paper challenges the standard method of measuring dataset bias by training classifiers, arguing that high accuracy often stems from resolution-based artifacts rather than true semantic differences. The authors demonstrate that these structural fingerprints, arising from native image resolutions and resizing, create robust, dataset-specific signatures that persist despite common augmentations. Their core contribution is showing that current bias metrics are frequently measuring these non-semantic artifacts, not meaningful semantic divergence.
arxiv.figure_caption: The ”Name That Dataset” game [ 36 ] aware of resolution distributions. These images are sampled from the YFCC and CC datasets. We maintain their relative sizes. Additionally, we provide a plot showing the resolution distribution of the training samples from each dataset. Can you guess which dataset each image is from? Once resolution effects are considered, dataset separability becomes significantly more apparent. (Answer: YFCC: 3, 5, 6, 8, 9; CC: 1, 2, 4, 7)

arxiv.title: Why MLLMs Struggle to Determine Object Orientations
arxiv.abstract: This paper investigates why Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) fail at 2D object orientation tasks by testing the hypothesis that visual encoders (like CLIP/SigLIP) lack geometric reasoning capabilities. The core method involves training linear regressors on encoder features (from models like LLaVA and Qwen) to predict object orientation, using both full images and rotated foreground patches. The contribution is the empirical finding that, contrary to the hypothesis, orientation information *is* preserved in the encoder embeddings, suggesting the failure lies elsewhere in the MLLM architecture.
arxiv.figure_caption: Set of images - Sets A and B are used for experiments with LLaVA-OV and Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct, and set C is used for LLaVA1.5 and LLaVA1.6. Since LLaVA-OV and Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct can be prompted with 2 images, real images were used. But since LLaVA1.5 and 1.6 can be prompted with only 1 image, synthetic images with reduced artifacts were used to improve the prediction accuracy. [A: Whole Images] : (a) dog (b) train (c) lizard (d) beach (e) indoor (f) fish. [B: In-Place Rotated Objects] : (a) koala on beach (b) vase on indoor (c) vase and toaster on indoor. [C: Blended Images with foreground on background] : (a) dog on beach (b) lizard on fish (c) train on indoor environment, with foreground scales 1, 2 and 3 from left to right.

arxiv.title: Context Sensitivity Improves Human-Machine Visual Alignment
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces a method for context-sensitive similarity computation using neural network embeddings, aiming to bridge the gap between fixed machine representations and human's context-adaptive processing. By incorporating an anchor image as simultaneous context in a triplet odd-one-out task, the approach achieves up to a 15% improvement in accuracy over context-insensitive models. This demonstrates that modeling context significantly enhances visual alignment, regardless of the underlying vision foundation model used.
arxiv.figure_caption: Qualitative comparison of context-insensitive and context-sensitive models. (a) Example triplet, where the human odd-one-out choice was made anchored in a given context. The context-sensitive model matches the human choice, while the context-insensitive model does not, reflecting the ambiguity of the triplet without context. (b) Representational similarity matrices (RSMs) over a reference image set, showing how the context-sensitive model modulates similarities given the context image in a. (c) PCA plot of the reference set and the three triplet images (same symbols as in a). The horse-cart image shifts to the animal cluster for the context-sensitive model.

arxiv.title: Decoding the Delta: Unifying Remote Sensing Change Detection and Understanding with Multimodal Large Language Models
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces **Delta-LLaVA**, a novel Multimodal Large Language Model framework designed to overcome the "temporal blindness" in remote sensing change detection. The core method employs a **Change-Enhanced Attention module** and a **Change-SEG module** to explicitly model and integrate multi-temporal visual differences with language understanding. The contribution is a unified approach for precise spatial grounding and cognitive-level change interpretation, validated by the new **Delta-QA benchmark**.
arxiv.figure_caption: Figure 1 . Our work primarily addresses the unified task of multi-temporal remote sensing change detection and understanding. The proposed Delta-QA dataset encompasses various QA task types and interaction modalities.

arxiv.title: Event-Adaptive State Transition and Gated Fusion for RGB-Event Object Tracking
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces MambaTrack, an RGB-Event object tracking framework that addresses static state transition issues in existing methods. Its core contribution is an **event-adaptive state transition mechanism** that dynamically adjusts the state transition matrix based on event stream density using a learnable scalar. Additionally, it employs a **Gated Projection Fusion (GPF) module** for robust cross-modal integration, generating adaptive gates from event density and RGB confidence.
arxiv.figure_caption: Illustration of the proposed DSSM module with the event-adaptive state transition mechanism. Based on the standard State Space Model (SSM), our DSSM module incorporates an event-adaptive state transition mechanism (highlighted in red), which dynamically modulates the transition dynamics according to the event stream, enabling better temporal modeling under varying event sparsity.

arxiv.title: Free Lunch for Unified Multimodal Models: Enhancing Generation via Reflective Rectification with Inherent Understanding
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces UniRect-CoT, a training-free framework that leverages the inherent, powerful understanding capabilities of Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) to improve their weaker generation performance. Inspired by human "Thinking-While-Drawing," the method treats the diffusion denoising process as visual reasoning, enabling the model to continuously reflect and rectify intermediate generation results using its existing knowledge. This "reflective rectification" unlocks a "free lunch" by activating dormant knowledge to enhance generative quality.
arxiv.figure_caption: An illustration of the capability mismatch in UMMs . The UMM fails to generate “purple pizza” as instructed, but it correctly identifies the color as “red” and accurately describes the intended target in the understanding task.

arxiv.title: Multi-Agent Object Detection Framework Based on Raspberry Pi YOLO Detector and Slack-Ollama Natural Language Interface
arxiv.abstract: This paper proposes a multi-agent object detection framework implemented on resource-constrained Raspberry Pi hardware using a YOLO detector. Its core contribution is demonstrating a fast prototyping approach that leverages an LLM-based natural language interface (Slack/Ollama) for system control and communication, moving beyond traditional specialized interfaces.

arxiv.title: POINTS-Seeker: Towards Training a Multimodal Agentic Search Model from Scratch
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces **POINTS-Seeker**, a novel approach to training a multimodal agentic search model entirely from scratch, rather than retrofitting existing LMMs. The core method involves an **Agentic Seeding** phase to establish foundational agentic behaviors, and the **V-Fold** compression scheme to overcome performance bottlenecks in long-horizon interactions by adaptively managing interaction history. This work contributes a new paradigm for building intrinsically agentic multimodal search systems.
arxiv.figure_caption: Overview of POINTS-Seeker. Our model adaptively interacts with external tools to solve complex, multi-hop VQA tasks. By leveraging V-Fold compression, stale history is rendered into compact visual tokens, effectively bypassing long-context performance degradation while achieving superior efficiency and reasoning fidelity.

arxiv.title: Remote Sensing Image Super-Resolution for Imbalanced Textures: A Texture-Aware Diffusion Framework
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces TexADiff, a novel diffusion framework specifically designed for remote sensing image super-resolution (RSISR) to overcome challenges posed by imbalanced textures. The core method involves estimating a Relative Texture Density Map (RTDM) which is then used to explicitly condition the diffusion process, modulate the loss function to prioritize texture-rich areas, and dynamically adapt the sampling schedule. This texture-aware conditioning significantly improves the model's spatial perception and detail synthesis in complex remote sensing imagery.
arxiv.figure_caption: Our method produces faithful, fine-grained details in the texture-rich ship regions, while avoiding stripe artifacts in the texture-sparse water regions.

arxiv.title: Seek-and-Solve: Benchmarking MLLMs for Visual Clue-Driven Reasoning in Daily Scenarios
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces **DailyClue**, a novel benchmark designed to evaluate Multimodal Large Language Models' (MLLMs) ability for **visual clue-driven reasoning** in authentic daily scenarios. The core method involves creating challenging questions grounded in real-world activities that force models to actively identify and leverage specific visual clues for complex reasoning, rather than relying on simple perception or pre-existing knowledge. This contributes a crucial evaluation tool that specifically targets and measures the critical reasoning capabilities often neglected by existing benchmarks.
arxiv.figure_caption: Overview of DailyClue. The left panel shows the hierarchical distribution (labels abbreviated for clarity), while the right panels detail the full taxonomy, data sources, and question types.

arxiv.title: Why Multimodal In-Context Learning Lags Behind? Unveiling the Inner Mechanisms and Bottlenecks
arxiv.abstract: This paper systematically analyzes why multimodal in-context learning (ICL) underperforms text-only ICL with few-shot examples. The authors decompose multimodal ICL into task mapping construction and transfer, revealing that current models suffer from a lack of reasoning-level alignment between visual and textual representations and struggle to reliably transfer learned task mappings to new queries. This work identifies the cross-modal alignment and transfer process as the key bottleneck limiting multimodal ICL performance.
arxiv.figure_caption: ( a–b ) Illustration of constructed outlier detection task Chen et al. ( 2025a ) , where (a) shows the zero-shot setup and (b) shows the few-shot setup. In the few-shot scenario (2-shot), the model must infer from the demonstrations whether the query should be solved based on shape or color, and then apply this inferred rule to identify the outlier item in either the image or the sentence. ( c ) Performance comparison of Qwen2.5-VL-7B and Gemma-3-12B, showing that multimodal ICL suffers a clear degradation under the few-shot setting (4-shot) relative to text-only ICL. ( d ) Illustration of the multimodal ICL pipeline, including task mapping construction from demonstrations and task mapping transfer to the query.

arxiv.title: MAny: Merge Anything for Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning
arxiv.abstract: The paper introduces **MAny** (Merge Anything), a framework to address catastrophic forgetting in Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning (MCIT) by tackling dual forgetting in both perception and reasoning spaces. MAny achieves this through **Cross-modal Projection Merging (CPM)** to recover visual alignment via prototype guidance, and **Low-rank Parameter Merging (LPM)** to mitigate interference among task-specific parameters. This dual-merging approach effectively preserves past knowledge while adapting to new multimodal tasks.
arxiv.figure_caption: Performance diagnosis on UCIT benchmark for LLaVA-1.5-7B and InternVL-Chat-7B during sequential LoRA fine-tuning. Tasks follow a clockwise sequence from Image-R.

arxiv.title: Out of Context: Reliability in Multimodal Anomaly Detection Requires Contextual Inference
arxiv.abstract: This paper addresses the unreliability of standard anomaly detection methods that assume a single, unconditional model of "normal" behavior. The core method introduces **contextual inference** to distinguish genuine anomalies from variations in operating conditions, recognizing that an observation's normalcy is context-dependent. This approach leverages multimodal data to build a more robust and context-aware reference model, significantly improving assessment reliability in dynamic environments.

arxiv.title: Inclusive Kitchen Design for Older Adults: Generative AI Visualizations to Support Mild Cognitive Impairment
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces a Generative AI system, leveraging Stable Diffusion with DreamBooth and ControlNet, to automatically transform standard kitchen photos into designs optimized for older adults with Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI). The system applies Home Design Guidelines to create visualizations featuring open layouts and better accessibility features. The core contribution is demonstrating that these AI-generated, cognitively-friendly kitchen visualizations are strongly preferred by users, offering a scalable design aid where professional help is limited.

arxiv.title: Jump-Start Reinforcement Learning with Vision-Language-Action Regularization
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces **Vision-Language-Action Jump-Starting (VLAJS)** to accelerate Reinforcement Learning (RL) for complex manipulation tasks. VLAJS uses a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model to provide transient, high-level action suggestions that regularize and guide the early exploration of an on-policy RL agent (PPO). This method effectively bridges the general reasoning of VLA models with the precise, high-frequency control of RL, significantly improving learning efficiency and exploration.

arxiv.title: Lazy or Efficient? Towards Accessible Eye-Tracking Event Detection Using LLMs
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces an accessible, code-free pipeline utilizing Large Language Models (LLMs) for eye-tracking event detection. The system translates natural language instructions into executable routines for data inspection, cleaning, and applying standard detectors to label fixations and saccades. This significantly lowers the barrier to entry for gaze analysis by automating complex preprocessing and parameterization steps.
arxiv.figure_caption: Figure 1. This figure compares our method with the traditional manual approach. In particular, the LLM-assisted workflow on the right illustrates our pipeline: (1) starting from raw data, the LLM automatically identifies relevant fields, assesses data quality characteristics, and generates an easy-to-understand summary; (2) users specify their requirements in natural language, and the LLM produces executable data-cleaning code together with parameterized implementations of selected event detectors (e.g., I-VT, I-DT); (3) the cleaned data is then passed to a detector set composed of multiple event-labeling algorithms; and (4) the results and explanatory reports are returned, allowing users to iteratively refine the code and parameters by editing prompts and rerunning the detection process.

arxiv.title: UMI-3D: Extending Universal Manipulation Interface from Vision-Limited to 3D Spatial Perception
arxiv.abstract: UMI-3D extends the wrist-mounted Universal Manipulation Interface (UMI) by integrating a low-cost LiDAR sensor to overcome the visual limitations of its predecessor. This enables robust, LiDAR-centric SLAM for accurate metric-scale pose estimation in challenging real-world manipulation scenarios. The core contribution is a synchronized multimodal sensing pipeline that produces consistent 3D representations for reliable data collection, significantly improving the quality of demonstrations for visuomotor policies.

arxiv.title: Enhancing Reinforcement Learning for Radiology Report Generation with Evidence-aware Rewards and Self-correcting Preference Learning
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces **ESC-RL**, a novel reinforcement learning framework for radiology report generation. It employs a **Group-wise Evidence-aware Alignment Reward (GEAR)** to provide fine-grained, evidence-grounded feedback, improving clinical faithfulness. Furthermore, it utilizes **Self-correcting Preference Learning (SPL)** to automatically build a reliable preference dataset and refine reports using an LLM, enabling self-improvement aligned with clinical standards.
arxiv.figure_caption: Overview of the proposed Evidence-aware Self-Correcting Reinforcement Learning (ESC-RL) framework.

arxiv.title: Multi-Task LLM with LoRA Fine-Tuning for Automated Cancer Staging and Biomarker Extraction
arxiv.abstract: This paper presents a parameter-efficient, multi-task framework using LoRA fine-tuning on Llama-3-8B-Instruct to automate the extraction of cancer staging (TNM), grade, and biomarkers from unstructured pathology reports. By employing parallel classification heads instead of purely generative methods, the model ensures schema adherence and effectively handles complex, heterogeneous report formats. The core contribution is achieving high accuracy (Macro F1 of 0.976) for structured data extraction while mitigating the computational cost and hallucination risks associated with large, fully fine-tuned LLMs.
arxiv.figure_caption: Detailed architecture of the proposed Multi-Task LLM framework. (a) Data Preprocessing: Raw pathology reports undergo native digital text extraction, de-identification, and human-verified ground truth generation before tokenization. (b) Data Splitting: The dataset is partitioned into training (80%), validation (10%), and testing (10%) sets to support model development and unbiased evaluation. (c) Model Architecture: A shared Llama-3 encoder (fine-tuned via LoRA) extracts high-dimensional semantic features from the terminal hidden state, which are simultaneously projected into parallel task-specific classification heads (T, N, M, Grade, ER, PR, HER2). (d) Optimization Loop: The training process minimizes a joint cross-entropy loss, updating only the low-rank adapters and head weights while keeping the heavy LLM backbone frozen. (e) Evaluation: The system produces a structured output vector containing the predicted class for each clinical variable, providing a comprehensive TNM and biomarker profile.

arxiv.title: MOONSHOT : A Framework for Multi-Objective Pruning of Vision and Large Language Models
arxiv.abstract: MOONSHOT is a framework that enhances post-training, one-shot model pruning by transforming single-objective methods into a multi-objective optimization. It jointly minimizes both the layer-wise reconstruction error and the second-order Taylor approximation of the training loss, addressing the inconsistency of using either objective alone. This wrapper approach provides a general and scalable way to improve pruning effectiveness across various model architectures and sparsity levels.

arxiv.title: Text-Attributed Knowledge Graph Enrichment with Large Language Models for Medical Concept Representation
arxiv.abstract: This paper introduces **CoMed**, an LLM-empowered framework to enrich medical knowledge graphs (KGs) for better medical concept representation. CoMed addresses limitations in existing KGs by integrating statistically derived, clinically relevant cross-type dependencies from EHRs with rich semantic information extracted from clinical text using Large Language Models. This dual integration results in higher-quality concept embeddings crucial for downstream clinical prediction tasks.
arxiv.figure_caption: Overview of CoMed . (1) Extract intra-visit co-occurrence and next-visit transition evidence from EHRs, and retain well-supported code pairs. (2) Use type-constrained, evidence-conditioned LLM prompting to assign directed relation types (with confidence and rationales), thereby constructing a heterogeneous KG.
